title: DFT-assisted natural abundance 13C zero-field NMR via optical magnetometry
authors: Blake Andrews, Xiao Liu, Raphael Zumbrunn, Calvin Lee, Sahand Adibnia, Emanuel Druga, Martin Head-Gordon, Ashok Ajoy
abstract: Zero-field (ZF) nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy probes scalar J-couplings between nuclei while dispensing with large homogeneous magnetic fields, enabling low-cost and geometrically flexible detection, including through conductive enclosures. Despite these advantages, its broader use for chemical analysis has been limited by sensitivity and by the difficulty of predicting the dense spectral multiplets that arise at zero field. Here we demonstrate natural-abundance (1.1%) 13C ZF spectroscopy on off-the-shelf liquids using a compact commercial 87Rb magnetometer for the first time, without hyperpolarization or special sample preparation. Instrumental advances yield improved sensitivity, <250-mHz linewidths and >week-long stability, enabling isotopomer-resolved fingerprint spectra across a 13-molecule library, including the ability to discern rare (0.0121%) doubly 13C-labelled species. In parallel, we demonstrate vibrationally corrected density-functional theory (DFT) based prediction of ZF NMR spectra for chemically diverse molecules with few-hertz accuracy. Comparing experiment with these calculations renders residual deviations as chemically informative, reporting on hydrogen bonding, hydration and ion pairing at high ionic strength. Together, these results contribute towards DFT-assisted ZF NMR as a general platform for field-constraint-free molecular identification and for extracting transient solution-state structure from responsive J-coupling observables.
summary: This paper presents a method for performing zero-field NMR spectroscopy on natural-abundance 13C without the need for large magnets or hyperpolarization. By using an 87Rb optical magnetometer, the researchers achieved high-sensitivity detection of molecular fingerprints. They also developed a DFT-based computational approach to predict these complex spectra accurately. This combination allows for the study of molecular environments, such as hydrogen bonding and ion pairing, through J-coupling observables.
main_problem: The use of zero-field (ZF) NMR for chemical analysis has been limited by low sensitivity and the difficulty of predicting dense spectral multiplets.
main_result: The authors demonstrate natural-abundance 13C ZF spectroscopy using an 87Rb magnetometer and show that vibrationally corrected DFT can predict these spectra with few-hertz accuracy.
method: The study combines experimental optical magnetometry using an 87Rb sensor with vibrationally corrected density-functional theory (DFT) calculations.
model_or_system: The experimental platform uses a compact commercial 87Rb magnetometer to probe scalar J-couplings in a 13-molecule library of off-the-shelf liquids at zero magnetic field.
key_observables: 13C zero-field NMR spectra, scalar J-couplings, and linewidths.
important_parameters: Natural abundance of 13C (1.1%), linewidths <250-mHz, and detection of rare doubly 13C-labelled species (0.0121%).
main_assumptions: The method assumes that vibrationally corrected DFT can accurately model the complex multiplets arising from J-couplings in the absence of an external field.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the advantages of ZF NMR, presents experimental results using Rb magnetometry, details the development of DFT-based spectral predictions, and demonstrates the utility of comparing experiment to theory for studying molecular structures.
why_interesting: This work is relevant to AMO physics due to the use of atomic vapor magnetometry (87Rb) for sensing and the study of molecular dynamics and J-coupling interactions in a field-free environment.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
